findings.title: Public oracle price setting allows manipulation of collateral valuation
findings.description: The ManipulableOracle contract's setPrice function has no access control, allowing anyone to set the price of any token to any value. FlashLoanVault uses this oracle to calculate collateral values via getTokenPrice(), which is called in multiple critical functions (calculateCollateralValue, borrow, removeCollateral, liquidate). An attacker can manipulate the price in the same transaction or via flash loan to artificially inflate or deflate collateral values.
findings.message: An attacker can drain all funds from the vault. For example: inflate their collateral price to borrow excessive amounts, or deflate others' collateral price to liquidate them and seize collateral at a discount.
findings.recommendation: Implement access control on setPrice (e.g., only owner). Use a decentralized oracle with TWAP (e.g., Chainlink) instead of a single-sourced, manipulable oracle. Add a circuit breaker or delay for price updates.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker takes a flash loan of a large amount of the quote token from a lending protocol.
2. Attacker dumps the borrowed token into the DEX pool, crashing the spot price.
3. Victim contract reads the manipulated spot price via getReserves() or similar — treats it as market price.
4. Attacker exploits the mispriced collateral: borrows against inflated collateral or liquidates positions unfairly.
5. Attacker swaps back to restore the price, repays the flash loan, and pockets the arbitrage profit.
6. Entire attack is atomic — completes within a single transaction with no capital at risk.

findings.title: Borrow function lacks flash loan state check allowing oracle manipulation attack
findings.description: The borrow function does not check the flashLoanLock state variable, so it can be called during the callback of a flash loan. Combined with the manipulable oracle, an attacker can take a flash loan, manipulate the oracle price to inflate their collateral value, and then borrow large amounts before restoring the price and repaying the flash loan.
findings.message: Attacker can borrow arbitrary amounts of tokens from the vault, extracting all available liquidity beyond the flash loan fee.
findings.recommendation: Add require(!flashLoanLock, 'No state change during flash loan') to the borrow function. Similarly protect other state-changing functions (deposit, withdraw, repay, addCollateral, removeCollateral).
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker has a small collateral position. 2. Attacker calls flashLoan to borrow all tokens from the vault. 3. In the callback (executeAttack), attacker sets oracle price to a very high value (e.g., 1e30) to inflate their collateral value. 4. Attacker calls borrow to borrow as much as possible (up to totalDeposits). 5. Attacker restores the oracle price. 6. Attacker repays the flash loan plus fee using some of the borrowed tokens. Net result: attacker gains the excess borrowed tokens beyond the flash loan fee.

findings.title: Permanent Denial of Service of Flash Loan Functionality
findings.description: The flashLoan function sets the `flashLoanLock` boolean to true before the external call and only resets it to false after successfully verifying the balance condition. If the balance check fails (e.g., receiver does not repay the fee, receiver is an EOA, or any reason causes the require to revert), the function reverts without resetting the lock. Since the lock is never cleared on revert, all future flash loan calls will be blocked indefinitely. This constitutes a critical denial-of-service vulnerability that can be triggered by any attacker, permanently disabling one of the protocol's core functions.
findings.recommendation: Add same-block protection (compare `block.number` at entry/exit). Use snapshot-based governance voting. Add timelock delays on sensitive operations.

findings.message: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.description: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.
findings.recommendation: Function assigns ownership but may not be the constructor — in Solidity <0.4.22, constructors were functions with the contract name. A typo means anyone can call it and become owner.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#59-83):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.flashLoan(uint256,address,bytes)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L59-L83):
	External call allowing reentrancy:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(receiver,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L69)
	Balance read before the call:
	- [balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(address(this))](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L63)
	Possible stale balance used after the call in a condition:
	- [require(bool,string)(balanceAfter >= balanceBefore + fee,Flash loan not repaid)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L79)
		- stale variable `balanceBefore`
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Repay updates state after external call allows reentrancy attack
findings.description: In the repay function, the token.transferFrom external call is executed before updating the internal accounting (borrowed[msg.sender] and totalBorrowed). If the token's transferFrom triggers a callback (e.g., ERC777 hooks or a malicious token), an attacker can reenter the vault and call functions that rely on the borrowed state, potentially causing accounting inconsistencies and allowing them to borrow more than their collateral allows.
findings.message: With a custom malicious token or a token that implements hooks (like ERC777), an attacker could exploit this reentrancy to borrow additional tokens against stale state, effectively draining the vault.
findings.recommendation: Apply the checks-effects-interactions pattern: update state (borrowed, totalBorrowed) before making the external transferFrom call. Alternatively, use a reentrancy guard modifier on all state-changing functions.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.description: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.
findings.recommendation: External send/transfer inside require — if recipient reverts, entire function is blocked (DoS). Use pull pattern instead.

findings.message: Event is missing `indexed` fields
findings.description: Index event fields make the field more quickly accessible to off-chain tools that parse events. However, note that each index field costs extra gas during emission, so it's not necessarily best to index the maximum allowed per event (three fields). Each event should use three indexed fields if there are three or more fields, and gas usage is not particularly of concern for the events in question. If there are fewer than three fields, all of the fields should be indexed.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Missing checks for `address(0)` when assigning values to address state variables
findings.description: Check for `address(0)` when assigning values to address state variables.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [FlashLoanAttacker](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L219-L252) has payable functions:
	 - [FlashLoanAttacker.receive()](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L251)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#157-165):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L157-L165):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L158)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [borrowed[msg.sender] -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L161)
	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).

3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [FlashLoanAttacker](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L219-L252) has payable functions:
	 - [FlashLoanAttacker.receive()](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L251)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#157-165):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.repay(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L157-L165):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L158)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [borrowed[msg.sender] -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L161)
	- [totalBorrowed -= amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L162)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.deposit(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#113-123):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.deposit(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L113-L123):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transferFrom(msg.sender,address(this),amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L115)
	State variables written after the call(s):
	- [deposits[msg.sender] += amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L119)
	- [totalDeposits += amount](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L120)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).

findings.message: Reentrancy in FlashLoanVault.withdraw(uint256) (../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#126-136):
findings.description:
Reentrancy in [FlashLoanVault.withdraw(uint256)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L126-L136):
	External calls:
	- [require(bool,string)(token.transfer(msg.sender,amount),Transfer failed)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L133)
	Event emitted after the call(s):
	- [Withdraw(msg.sender,amount)](../data/audit/FlashLoanVault.sol#L135)
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
